thesis: AMD is the clear number-two in AI accelerators and the primary credible challenger to NVIDIA's data-center GPU monopoly. FY2025 delivered record revenue of $34.6B (+34% YoY) with Data Center revenue of $16.6B (+32%), and the Instinct MI350 ramp plus the MI400 series and Helios rack-scale platform in 2026 give AMD a genuine shot at meaningful GPU share as customers seek a second source. Seven of the ten largest AI companies — including OpenAI, Meta, and xAI — now use Instinct GPUs. The problem is price: at ~$496 the stock trades near 187x GAAP EPS and roughly 45–55x forward non-GAAP earnings, embedding near-flawless MI400 execution. Strong thesis, extreme valuation — hence HOLD pending a better entry or proof of the 2026 GPU ramp.

drivers: MI400 series and Helios rack-scale system ramp hard through 2026, roughly doubling Data Center GPU revenue growth versus 2025
AMD captures double-digit share of the merchant AI-accelerator market as OpenAI, Meta, xAI, Microsoft and Oracle scale Instinct deployments
ROCm software maturity and open-standard positioning erode NVIDIA's CUDA lock-in enough to win a durable second-source slot at every major buyer
EPYC Venice (Zen 6, up to 256 cores) extends server CPU share gains above 40% and lifts blended data-center margins
Non-GAAP EPS compounds toward $12–15 by 2027 and the stock holds a premium AI-compute multiple

base_case.assumptions: MI350 continues shipping and MI400/Helios launch on schedule in 2026 with solid but not NVIDIA-displacing volumes. Data Center revenue grows 40–60% in 2026; Client and Embedded grow steadily; Gaming stays soft. Gross margin drifts toward 52–54% on mix and GPU scale. The extreme multiple compresses modestly as growth is delivered rather than merely anticipated, leaving the stock range-bound-to-higher. 2026 revenue of $48–55B with non-GAAP EPS of $8–10.

risks: MI400 underdelivers against NVIDIA's Rubin generation on performance, availability, or software, and the second-source thesis stalls
AI-capex digestion in 2026–2027 slows hyperscaler GPU orders and channel inventory builds
Gross margins stay stuck near 50% as GPU pricing is used aggressively to buy share from NVIDIA
The ~187x GAAP / 45–55x forward multiple compresses sharply toward semiconductor-peer levels on any growth wobble
Customer concentration and export-control exposure to China weigh on the Data Center trajectory

catalysts.event: Q2 2026 earnings — MI350 ramp, MI400 pre-orders, and 2026 Data Center GPU guidance in focus
Instinct MI400 series (MI430X / MI440X / MI455X) production and Helios double-wide rack launch (up to 3 AI exaflops per rack)
EPYC Venice (Zen 6, up to 256 cores) server CPU launch driving continued share gains
Q3 2026 earnings — first full quarter of MI400 revenue contribution
CES 2027 keynote — client AI, next-gen roadmap and MI500 details

key_risks: Software moat gap: NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem remains the industry default; ROCm adoption must keep accelerating for the GPU thesis to hold
Valuation risk at ~$800B market cap, ~187x GAAP EPS and ~45–55x forward non-GAAP earnings — priced for near-flawless MI400 execution
Customer concentration: a handful of hyperscalers and AI labs drive Data Center GPU demand; any capex pause hits AMD disproportionately
Competition from NVIDIA (Rubin), custom ASICs from Broadcom and Marvell, and a resurgent Intel across CPU and accelerator markets
TSMC supply-chain and advanced-packaging (CoWoS) dependency; leading-edge and packaging capacity are shared bottlenecks
U.S.–China export controls on advanced AI chips continue to constrain the addressable Data Center GPU market
